back_translation: In that time, when the disciples were multiplying in number, there arose a grumbling of the Jews-who-spoke-the-Greek-language against the Jews-who-spoke-the-Hebrew-language, that their widows were being overlooked in the daily distribution.
So the Twelve apostles called the group of all the disciples together, and said, "It is not fitting for us to abandon the word of God in order to go serve at tables of food.
Therefore, brothers, select seven men from among you who are attested, full of the Spirit and of wisdom, so that we will assign this duty to them.
As for us, we will devote ourselves continuously to prayer, and to the ministry of the word."

This word pleased the whole group of disciples, and they selected Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit, along with Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolas an Antiochian-proselyte.
They brought these seven to stand before the apostles, and then the apostles prayed and laid hands on them.
The word of God grew, and the number of disciples in Jerusalem multiplied exceedingly, and a great number of priests became obedient to the faith.
As for Stephen, full of grace and power, he was doing great wonders and signs among the people.
But some from the synagogue called "Libertine," including Cyrenians, Alexandrians, and men from the province of Cilicia and the province of Asia, rose up disputing with Stephen.
But they were not able to oppose the wisdom and the Spirit by which he was speaking.
Then they secretly-instigated some men to say, "We have heard him speaking blasphemous words against Moses and against God."
They stirred up the people, the elders, and the scribes, then came upon and seized Stephen, bringing him to the Sanhedrin.
And they brought false witnesses saying, "This man speaks unceasingly against this holy place and the law.
For we have heard him say that this Jesus of Nazareth will destroy this place and will change the various customs that Moses handed down to us."
All who were sitting in the Sanhedrin gazed intently at Stephen, and saw his face as if like the face of an angel.
